---
title: "Exploring Variables"
subtitle: "Scales, Data Types and Distributions"
format: revealjs
footer: "Exploring Variables - Scales, Data Types and Distributions"
---

```{r}
#| include: false
source("../setup.R")
```

## Overview

- Loading Data from CSV
- Scales of Measurement
- Data Types in R
- Nominal Scale
- Ordinal Scale
- Interval Scale
- Ratio Scale

# Loading Data from CSV

## With `{readr}`

The `readr` package provides functions to read data from text files.

```{r}
#| eval: false
survey <- read_csv("data/mds12_schoko_milch.csv")
```

## A Look at the Result

```{r}
survey
```


## Data Representation in R

![](/images/slides/google_slides/data_representation_in_r.svg)

## Dimensions of the Data

Number of observations and variables:

```{r}
dim(survey)

num_cols <- ncol(survey)
num_rows <- nrow(survey)

print(str_glue("The data set has {num_rows} rows and {num_cols} columns."))
```

## Adding an ID Column

If there is no ID, we can add a surrogate ID:

```{r}
survey <- survey |> 
  rowid_to_column("id")
```

# Scales of Measurement

## Four Scales of Measurement

![](/images/slides/variable-scales.svg)

::: aside
Read more on scales of measurement in @doring_forschungsmethoden_2023, chapter 8.4
:::

## Types of Variables

![](/images/slides/types-of-variables-with-data-types.svg)

::: aside
See @effect_2026, chapter 3
:::

# Data Types in R

## Overview of Data Types

| Type           | Description                     | Example                        |
|----------------|---------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| `<chr>`        | String of characters            | `"hello"`     |
| `<int>`        | Whole number                    | `42L`                          |
| `<dbl>`        | Decimal number                  | `3.14`                         |
| `<lgl>`        | Boolean value                   | `TRUE` or `FALSE`              |
| `<fct>`        | Fixed set of values             | `male`, `female`, `diverse`    |

## Finding the Data Type

```{r}
survey |> 
    glimpse()
```

## Selecting by Data Type

```{r}
survey |> 
    select(where(is.character)) |> 
    names()
```

# Nominal Scale

## Nominal Scale Overview

Categorical variables without a natural order.

::: incremental
-   **Possible interpretations**: Two values are the same or different
-   **Examples**: gender, religion, political affiliation
-   **Data Type**:  convert to `<fct>` 
:::

## Converting to Factor {.smaller}

Nominal variables often come as character strings. We can convert them to factors:

```{r}
survey |> 
  select(q004geschlecht) |> 
  mutate(q004geschlecht = as_factor(q004geschlecht))
```

## Recoding Values {.smaller}

We can recode the values to something more meaningful:

```{r}
s_q004 <- 
    survey |>
        select(q004geschlecht) |> 
        mutate(q004geschlecht = 
            as_factor(q004geschlecht) |>
            fct_recode(
                "männlich" = "1",
                "weiblich" = "2",
                "divers" = "3"
            )
        )

s_q004 |> head(5)
```

## Frequencies of Nominal Variables

```{r}
s_q004 |> 
    count(q004geschlecht)
```

## Using a Bar Plot

```{r}
s_q004 |> 
    ggplot() +
    aes(x = q004geschlecht) +
    geom_bar()
```

## Order by Frequency

```{r}
s_q004 |> 
    ggplot() +
    aes(x = fct_infreq(q004geschlecht)) +
    geom_bar()
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables

Define a vector of variable names:

```{r}
locations_labels <- c(
  "v008ort_1discount" = "Discounter",
  "v008ort_2super" = "Supermarkt",
  "v008ort_3sbmarkt" = "SB-Markt",
  "v008ort_4fach" = "Fachgeschäft",
  "v008ort_5markt" = "Wochenmarkt",
  "v008ort_6hof" = "Hofladen",
  "v008ort_7bioladen" = "Bioladen",
  "v008ort_8onlineds" = "Online (Lieferdienst)",
  "v008ort_9online" = "Online (Shop)",
  "v008ort_other" = "Sonstiges"
)
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables

We can access the label of a variable:

```{r}
locations_labels["v008ort_1discount"]
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables {auto-animate="true"}

Get all variables belonging to question 8...

```{r}
#| output: false
survey |> 
  select(id, contains("008"))
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables {auto-animate="true"}

But not the "other" variable...

```{r}
#| output: false
survey |> 
  select(id, contains("008"), -v008ort_other)
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables {auto-animate="true"}

Make the data long for easier analysis...

```{r}
#| output: false
survey |> 
  select(id, contains("008"), -v008ort_other) |>
  pivot_longer(
      cols = c(everything(), -id), 
      names_to = "location", 
      values_to = "mentioned"
  )
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables {auto-animate="true"}

Convert to factor using the labels...

```{r}
#| output: false
survey |> 
  select(id, contains("008"), -v008ort_other) |>
  pivot_longer(
      cols = c(everything(), -id), 
      names_to = "location", 
      values_to = "mentioned"
  ) |> 
  mutate(location = as_factor(locations_labels[location]))
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables {auto-animate="true"}

Filter only the mentioned locations...

```{r}
#| output: false
survey |> 
  select(id, contains("008"), -v008ort_other) |>
  pivot_longer(
      cols = c(everything(), -id), 
      names_to = "location", 
      values_to = "mentioned"
  ) |> 
  mutate(location = as_factor(locations_labels[location])) |> 
  filter(mentioned == 1)
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables {auto-animate="true"}

Count how often each location was mentioned...

```{r}
#| output: false
survey |> 
  select(id, contains("008"), -v008ort_other) |>
  pivot_longer(
      cols = c(everything(), -id), 
      names_to = "location", 
      values_to = "mentioned"
  ) |> 
  mutate(location = as_factor(locations_labels[location])) |> 
  filter(mentioned == 1) |> 
  count(location)
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables {auto-animate="true"}

Finally, calculate relative frequencies...

```{r}
survey |> 
  select(id, contains("008"), -v008ort_other) |>
  pivot_longer(
      cols = c(everything(), -id), 
      names_to = "location", 
      values_to = "mentioned"
  ) |> 
  mutate(location = as_factor(locations_labels[location])) |> 
  filter(mentioned == 1) |> 
  count(location) |> 
  mutate(anteil = n / num_rows) |> 
  head(3)
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables {auto-animate="true"}

Store this in a new tibble...

```{r}
s_q008_agg <- survey |> 
  select(id, contains("008"), -v008ort_other) |>
  pivot_longer(
      cols = c(everything(), -id), 
      names_to = "location", 
      values_to = "mentioned"
  ) |> 
  mutate(location = as_factor(locations_labels[location])) |> 
  filter(mentioned == 1) |> 
  count(location) |> 
  mutate(anteil = n / num_rows)
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables {auto-animate="true"}

And visualize it...

```{r}
s_q008_agg  |> 
  ggplot() +
  aes(y = location, x = anteil) +
  geom_col()
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables {auto-animate="true"}

Order the bars by frequencies...

```{r}
s_q008_agg  |> 
  ggplot() +
  aes(y = fct_reorder(location, n), x = anteil) +
  geom_col()
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables {auto-animate="true"}

Add a nice label to the x-axis with percentages...

```{r}
s_q008_agg  |> 
  ggplot() +
  aes(y = fct_reorder(location, n), x = anteil) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format())
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables {auto-animate="true"}

Add a header and axis labels...

```{r}
s_q008_agg  |> 
  ggplot() +
  aes(y = fct_reorder(location, n), x = anteil) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Wo kaufen die Menschen ein?", x = "Anteil der Nennungen", y = NULL)
```

## Multiple Nominal Variables {auto-animate="true"}

Use a lighter theme...

```{r}
s_q008_agg  |> 
  ggplot() +
  aes(y = fct_reorder(location, n), x = anteil) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(title = "Wo kaufen die Menschen ein?", x = "Anteil der Nennungen", y = NULL) +
  theme_light()
```

# Ordinal Scale

## Ordinal Scale Overview

Categorical variables with a natural order, but no fixed intervals.

::: incremental
-   **Possible interpretations**: One value is greater than another value
-   **Examples**: school grades, satisfaction ratings, t-shirt sizes
-   **Data Type**:  convert to `<fct>` (ordered)
:::


## Multiple Choice Answers {.smaller}

Get all variables belonging to question 11:

```{r}
survey |> 
  select(id, contains("011"))
```

::: aside
This works if the data collection was designed thoughtfully.
:::

## Ordered Factors

"Ich habe ein höheres Vertrauen in regionale Lebensmittel"

```{r}
s_q011_vertrauen <- survey |> 
    select(p011regio_2vertrauen) |> 
    mutate(p011regio_2vertrauen = 
        as_factor(p011regio_2vertrauen) |> 
        fct_recode(
            "trifft nicht zu" = "-2",
            "trifft eher nicht zu" = "-1",
            "teils-teils" = "0",
            "trifft eher zu" = "1",
            "trifft voll zu" = "2"
        ) |> 
        fct_relevel("trifft nicht zu", "trifft eher nicht zu", "teils-teils", "trifft eher zu", "trifft voll zu")
    )
```

## Frequencies of Ordinal Variables

Automatically ordered by the factor levels:

```{r}
s_q011_vertrauen |> 
    count(p011regio_2vertrauen)
```

## Using a Bar Plot

No need to order the bars manually:

```{r}
s_q011_vertrauen |> 
    ggplot() +
    aes(x = p011regio_2vertrauen) +
    geom_bar()
```

# Interval Scale

## Interval Scale Overview

Ordered values with fixed intervals, but no true zero point.

::: incremental
-   **Possible interpretations**: The difference between two values is the same (or not) as the difference between two other values
-   **Examples**: temperature in Celsius or Fahrenheit, calendar years
-   **Data Type**:  `<int>` or `<dbl>`
:::

## Year of Birth

```{r}
s_q002birthyear <-
  survey |> 
  select(q002geburt) |> 
  mutate(q002geburt = as.integer(q002geburt))

s_q002birthyear |> 
    head(5)
```

## Summary Statistics

```{r}
s_q002birthyear |> 
    drop_na() |>
    summarise(
        min = min(q002geburt),
        q25 = quantile(q002geburt, 0.25),
        median = median(q002geburt),
        q75 = quantile(q002geburt, 0.75),
        max = max(q002geburt),
        mean = mean(q002geburt)
    )
```

## With `skimr`

Get info as a tibble:

```{r}
library(skimr)
s_q002birthyear |>
  skim(q002geburt) |> 
  as_tibble() |> 
  clean_names()
```

## With `summarytools` {.smaller}

Only for quick info in the console:

```{r}
library(summarytools)
s_q002birthyear |>
  dfSummary()
```

## Histogram

Distribution of birth years:

```{r}
s_q002birthyear |> 
    ggplot() +
    aes(x = q002geburt) +
    geom_histogram(binwidth = 5)
```

## Boxplot

Distribution of birth years with a different focus:

```{r}
s_q002birthyear |> 
    ggplot() +
    aes(x = q002geburt) +
    geom_boxplot()
```

# Ratio Scale

## Ratio Scale Overview

Ordered values with fixed intervals and a true zero point.

::: incremental
-   **Possible interpretations**: The ratio of two values is meaningful (e.g., one value is twice as much as another)
-   **Examples**: weight, height, income, age
-   **Data Type**:  `<int>` or `<dbl>`
:::

## Accepted Price

"Geben Sie [..] bitte den Preis an, den Sie für angemessen halten und bei dem Sie zugreifen
würden!"

```{r}
s_q013_accepted_price <- survey |> 
    select(u013pzahl_1weihen) |> 
    transmute(accepted_price = as.double(u013pzahl_1weihen))

s_q013_accepted_price |> 
    drop_na() |>
    head(5)
```

## Summary Statistics

Look at the mean (!)

```{r}
s_q013_accepted_price |> 
    drop_na() |>
    summarise(
        min = min(accepted_price),
        q25 = quantile(accepted_price, 0.25),
        median = median(accepted_price),
        q75 = quantile(accepted_price, 0.75),
        max = max(accepted_price),
        mean = mean(accepted_price)
    )
```

## Ratios Become Meaningful

How much more or less is the respondent willing to pay compared to the median?

```{r}
s_q013_accepted_price |> 
    drop_na() |>
    mutate(
        median_price = median(accepted_price),
        ratio_to_median = accepted_price / median_price * 100,
        above_median = accepted_price > median_price
    ) |> 
    head(5)
```

## References
